source_metadata.title_raw: Caren Ray Russom -  A-19-125 - October 29, 2019 - Arroyo Grande

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 29, 2019

Caren Ray Russom
Mayor, City of Arroyo Grande
300 E Branch Street
Arroyo Grande, CA 93420

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-125

Dear Ms. Russom:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of Government Code Section 1090.¹ Please note that we are not a finder of fact when tendering advice and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71)

Under Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Luis Obispo County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

1. May the City Council for the City of Arroyo Grande (the City) exercise an option to extend a contract with a consulting firm where the Mayor is married to an employee of the consulting firm?

2. Is the Mayor required to recuse herself from participating in the decision regarding the City's option to extend a contract with a consulting firm where the Mayor is married to an employee of the consulting firm?

CONCLUSION

1. Yes. The City Council may exercise the option to extend the contract as long as the Mayor discloses her financial interest in the contract and the disclosure is noted in the official records, and the Mayor abstains from any participation in making the contract.

2. Yes. The mayor is prohibited from taking part in the decisions. To comply with the Act's conflict of interest recusal requirements, the Mayor must announce her interest and leave the room for the duration of the discussion and the vote on the option to extend the contract.

¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-125
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

A.     Background.

You were elected and served as a member of the City Council for the City from December 2016, to December 2018. You were elected and sworn in as Mayor of the City in December of 2018. In August of 2018, you married Randy Russom who is an employee of RRM Design Group (RRM). RRM has provided consulting services to the City since 1997.

In July of 2017, the City entered an Agreement for consultant services with RRM (the Agreement). You were not married to Mr. Russom at that time. The Agreement is for consulting services to prepare an East Grand Avenue Master Plan (the Master Plan) and is set to terminate on July 25, 2019. RRM has been preparing the Master Plan for the past two years. It is approximately 50% completed. Section 1 of the Agreement contains a provision that the Agreement may be extended for two additional one-year periods. City staff is recommending that the City extend the Agreement with RRM in order to allow RRM to complete the Master Plan. The estimated project total contained in RRM's original proposal is for $217,871. Mr. Russom did not participate in formulating the original proposal for the Agreement and is not assigned to this project.

The Agreement was procured under a competitive selection process. A Request for Proposals (RFP) was issued by the Community Development Department. The City anticipated that it would receive multiple proposals, but RRM was the only firm to submit a proposal. The City conducted two rounds of RFPs. The City issued its first RFP on May 8, 2015, sent directly to 29 potential firms and posted on the City's website. The RFP was available for three weeks and closed on May 29, 2015. The City did not receive a single proposal. The City released a second RFP on April 27, 2017, which was again sent to the 29 potential firms, but with additional information on the intended scope of the project and a longer, five-week response timeline. The proposal received from RRM and its sub-consultant team was the only proposal the City received.

B.     Mr. Russom and RRM.

RRM is a design firm composed of over 130 employees, including architects, engineers, landscape architects, planners, and surveyors in four offices across California. RRM is a 100% employee-owned company. Mr. Russom is an architect and has been an employee of RRM for six years. His job title is Manager of Architecture, and he is not an RRM officer or director. He serves in a primary management capacity and owns 0% of the shares of stock in RRM.

Mr. Russom participates in RRM's Employee Stock Ownership Plan (ESOP). An ESOP is an employee benefit plan, similar to a 401(k) or a pension benefit plan. The ESOP assets, in this case the RRM stock, are held under the ESOP trust agreement for the benefit of ESOP participants. The ESOP trustee is the legal owner of the stock held under the ESOP trust agreement, the holder of record of the stock, and is the "shareholder" as defined under Section 185 of the California Corporations Code. The ESOP trustee, as the legal owner and shareholder, has the power to vote the shares, including the power to vote for the election of the corporation's directors.

File No. A-19-125
Page No. 3

An ESOP participant like Mr. Russom is not an equity owner of the business. A participant does not have power to vote on ESOP assets, is not shareholder, and is not the holder of record. Each participant has an individual account for administration and recordkeeping. ESOP assets are allocated to participant accounts. The value of each participant's individual account is based on the value of allocated plan assets in the account which is determined by the current value of the employer stock. This beneficial interest does not constitute an ownership interest in the business.

ANALYSIS

A. The Act.

1. Economic interest in a source of income.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. Section 87103 defines financial interests to include:

• Any business entity in which the public official, or member of the official's immediate family including a spouse, has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. (Sections 82034, 82029, and 87103(a).)

• Any source of income, including your community property interest in you spouse's income, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Sections 82030 and 87103(c).)

Based on the facts provided, it appears you have an indirect investment interest in RRM worth $2,000 or more resulting from your spouse's beneficial interest in shares of the company owned by the ESOP. You also appear to have a community property interest in your spouse's income from RRM that will exceed $500 in any 12-month period. Accordingly, you have interests in RRM as both a business entity and a source of income.

2. Reasonably foreseeable and materiality.

The next issue is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial effect on your financial interest. Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in the decision. An official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision.

Similarly, a reasonably foreseeable material effect on an official's interest in a business entity including a source of income is material any time the interest is explicitly involved in the decision including any decision in which the business entity bids on, or enters into, a contract with the agency, or is identified as a subcontractor on a bid or contract with the agency, as well as any

File No. A-19-125
Page No. 4

decision in which the entity is the subject of any action taken by the agency that is directed at the
entity. (Regulations 18702.1(a) and 18702.3(a)(4).)

In this case, RRM has an existing contract with the City, which has a prospective decision to
extend the existing RRM contract to complete the Master Plan. Based on the facts provided, RRM
is explicitly involved in this decision. Thus, the financial effect of the decision on RRM is both
foreseeable and material, and the Act prohibits you from taking part in the decision.

We also note that when a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has
a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior
to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision
as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or
herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section
87105.)

B.      Section 1090.

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v.
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest." (People v. Home (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) A contract is typically "made" on mutual assent of the involved parties. (Stigall, supra, at p.
569.) Making or participating in making a contract has been broadly construed to include instances
where a public official has influence over the contract or its terms. (See 80 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 41.)

1. Contract Amendments, Extensions or Renewals.

While the contract preexisted the Mayor's marriage to an RRM employee, the pertinent
question is whether the Mayor has a prohibited financial interest in any future changes to the
contract, including any extensions or renewals of the terms of that contract. A Government Code
section 1090 violation exists when: (1) an official participates in the making of a contract in his or
her official capacity; (2) the official has a cognizable financial interest in the contract; and (3) that
financial interest does not fall within one of the exceptions for "remote" interests or minimal
"noninterests" provided in Sections 1091 and 1091.5.

Changes to existing contracts including amendments, extensions, and renewals are
"contracts" under section 1090. When members of a public board, commission or similar body have
the power to execute contracts, each member is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making

File No. A-19-125
Page No. 5

of all contracts by his or her agency regardless of whether the member actually participates in the
making of the contract. (Thomson v. Call, supra at pp. 645, 649; Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. City
of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201, 211; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49 (2006).) Thus, as a matter of
law, the public official would be participating in the making of a contract in her official capacity if
the City were to amend, extend, or renew its contract with RRM.

The next issue is whether the official has a financial interest in the contract. In determining
whether a financial interest exists under Section 1090, courts "generally focus on whether the
contract in question could confer some type of pecuniary advantage to the target of a Section 1090
inquiry." (Eden Township Healthcare District v. Sutter Health (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 208, 225.) A
member of a board or commission always has a financial interest in his spouse's source of income
for purposes of Section 1090. (78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 235 (1995).) A married official "stands in
the shoes of his spouse." (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 258, 264 (2006).) In this case, the Mayor has a
financial interest in any future contract between the City and RRM.

2. Statutory exceptions.

The final question is whether a statutory exception applies to the Mayor's financial interest
in the RRM contract. Under Section 1091(b)(3), the interest of an employee or agent of the
contracting party is a "remote interest" if all of the following conditions are met:

(A)	The agency of which the person is an officer is a local public agency located in a
county with a population of less than 4,000,000.
(B)	The contract is competitively bid and is not for personal services.
(C)	The employee or agent is not in a primary management capacity with the contracting
party, is not an officer or director of the contracting party and holds no ownership
interest in the contracting party.
(D)	The contracting party has 10 or more other employees.
(E)	The employee or agent did not directly participate in formulating the bid of the
contracting party.
(F)	The contracting party is the lowest responsible bidder.

Based on the facts provided, each of the foregoing requirements for a remote interest under
Section 1091(b)(3) are satisfied. The City is located in San Luis Obispo County with a population
of less than 4,000,000. The Agreement was competitively bid and was for professional consulting
services, not personal services. The City issued RFPs on two occasions for the project and RRM
was both the only bidder and the lowest responsible bidder.2

RRM is a design firm with over 130 employees in multiple offices in California and is 100%
employee-owned company. Mr. Russom has been an employee of RRM for six years and is an
RRM employee, not an officer or director. He is an architect and a Manager of Architecture for
RRM. He serves in a primary management capacity and owns 0% of the shares of stock in RRM.
As a participant in RRM's ESOP plan, Mr. Russom is not an equity owner of the business. He does

2 The decision to exercise the option arises from the initial contract that was competitively bid. Accordingly,
we find that the remote interest exception provided in 1091(b)(3) extends beyond the entering of a contract and may
also apply to subsequent decisions necessitated by the competitively bid contract.

File No. A-19-125
Page No. 6

not have power to vote on ESOP assets, he is not shareholder, and he is not the holder of record. The ESOP trust owns the shares. Thus, Mr. Russom's beneficial interest in the ESOP plan does not constitute an ownership interest in RRM.

Accordingly, you will have a statutory "remote interest" under Section 1091(b)(3) in the Agreement. When a "remote interest" exists, a board may execute a contract as long as: (1) the officer discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency, (2) such interest is noted in the entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the making of the contract. (Section 1091(a); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).)

For the reasons stated above, the City Council may exercise the option to extend the Agreement as long as you disclose your financial interest in the agreement, that disclosure is noted in the official records, and you abstain from any participation in making the agreement. Also, to comply with the Act's conflict of interest recusal requirements, you must leave the room for the duration of the discussion and the vote on the option to extend the Agreement.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: John M. Feser Jr.
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JMF:aja

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
May the City Council for the City of Arroyo Grande (the City) exercise an option to 
extend a contract with a consulting firm where the Mayor is married to an employee of the 
consulting firm? 
2. 
Is the Mayor required to recuse herself from participating in tHe decision regarding 
the City's option to extend a contract with a consulting firm where the Mayor is married to an 
employee of the consulting firm?

CONCLUSION: 1. 
Yes. The City Council may exercise the option to extend the contract as long as the 
Mayor discloses her financial interest in the contract and the disclosure is noted in the official 
records, and the Mayor abstains from any participation in making the contract. 
2. 
Yes. The mayor is prohibited from taking part in the decisions. To comply with the 
Act's conflict of interest recusal requirements, the Mayor must announc,e her interest and leave the 
room for the duration of the discussion and the vote on the option to extend the contract. 
1 FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
A.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 October 29, 2019 Caren Ray Russom Mayor, City of Arroyo Grande 300 E Branch Street Arroyo Grande, CA 93420 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-125 Dear Ms. Russom: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of Government Code Section 1090.¹ Please note that we are not a finder of fact when tendering advice and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71) Under Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Luis Obispo County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTION 1. May the City Council for the City of Arroyo Grande (the City) exercise an option to extend a contract with a consulting firm where the Mayor is married to an employee of the consulting firm? 2. Is the Mayor required to recuse herself from participating in the decision regarding the City's option to extend a contract with a consulting firm where the Mayor is married to an employee of the consulting firm? CONCLUSION 1. Yes. The City Council may exercise the option to extend the contract as long as the Mayor discloses her financial interest in the contract and the disclosure is noted in the official records, and the Mayor abstains from any participation in making the contract. 2. Yes. The mayor is prohibited from taking part in the decisions. To comply with the Act's conflict of interest recusal requirements, the Mayor must announce her interest and leave the room for the duration of the discussion and the vote on the option to extend the contract. ¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-125 Page No. 2 FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR A. Background. You were elected and served as a member of the City Council for the City from December 2016, to December 2018. You were elected and sworn in as Mayor of the City in December of 2018. In August of 2018, you married Randy Russom who is an employee of RRM Design Group (RRM). RRM has provided consulting services to the City since 1997. In July of 2017, the City entered an Agreement for consultant services with RRM (the Agreement). You were not married to Mr. Russom at that time. The Agreement is for consulting services to prepare an East Grand Avenue Master Plan (the Master Plan) and is set to terminate on July 25, 2019. RRM
